articles.full_text:
Depression in Arabian Sea pushes Rajasthan temperatures up:Barmer nears 39°C while nights remain unusually cool
- Copy link
Heat has intensified across Rajasthan due to the formation of an anti-cyclone in the Arabian Sea. Strong hot winds blowing from the west have brought scorching conditions to the state’s border districts. On Wednesday, the maximum temperature in Barmer approached 39°C.
Temperatures also touched around 35°C in Jaipur, Ajmer, Churu and Udaipur. However, in contrast to the hot days, nights remain pleasantly cool, with many cities recording minimum temperatures below 15°C.
The Jaipur Meteorological Centre has predicted a further rise in temperatures across the state over the next two to three days. As a result, an alert has been issued for hot winds in the western districts.
Barmer recorded the highest temperature
During the last 24 hours, skies remained clear across all districts. Several cities experienced light warm winds in the afternoon along with strong sunshine. Maximum temperatures above 35°C were recorded in Udaipur, Sriganganagar, Kota, Jodhpur, Jaisalmer, Jaipur, Churu, Bikaner and Ajmer.
Barmer was the hottest, where the maximum temperature was recorded at 38.8°C.
Cool nights, temperature below 10°C
Despite the intense daytime heat, evenings are bringing relief. In several cities, temperatures drop sharply at night, with minimum temperatures falling below 15°C.
Pali recorded the coldest night on Wednesday, with the minimum temperature at 8.8°C. Fatehpur (Sikar) recorded 11°C, while Karauli recorded 11.4°C.
Why did the temperature rise suddenly?
Weather experts say the main reason for the rising temperatures is the formation of an anti-cyclone over the Arabian Sea near the Gujarat–Pakistan region.
In an anti-cyclone system, winds rotate clockwise and descend rapidly from higher levels of the atmosphere towards the surface. This creates high pressure and keeps the sky completely clear.
Because of this system, winds circulating through Pakistan are now entering Rajasthan directly. As a result, temperatures are rising not only in Rajasthan but also in Gujarat, Maharashtra and Madhya Pradesh.
